body_txt: Alice encounters a doppelgänger or a recording of herself and is shocked as to the style of speech she uses, and how stupid it sounds, and inquires of her friends as to whether she really sounds like that and/or says those things all the time. Truth in Television — or so it's often claimed, in a phenomenon called "Voice Confrontation." When we hear another person's voice, the sound waves travel only through the air. However, our voice is produced by own vocal cords, meaning in addition to hearing the sounds issued from the mouth, we hear sounds that reverberate in our own body. Because bone and flesh are better conductors of lower frequencies, our voice generally sounds "richer" and "deeper" to us. Hearing a recording of our voice is like listening to another person, meaning it sounds quite different. But the primary reason our recorded voice sounds "bad" or "wrong" is we're simply not used to it. Participants in a study gave higher ratings to a voice's attractiveness when they didn't recognize it was their own. For the same reason, some people claim they "look worse in photographs." This is because we're used to seeing ourselves in mirrors, where the image is horizontally flipped, creating a subtle but noticeable effect when we see our non-flipped selves (i.e. how everyone else sees us). Again, because we're not used to it, it creates a feeling of unfamiliarity and "wrongness." Compare with Reality Is Unrealistic . Contrast with Voice Change Surprise . Here is a compilation with a lot of examples of this quote and other variations.
